narrative:
irst Warn Forecast: Saturday, May 16, 2026
First Warn Headlines
Scattered showers and thunderstorms continue this morning across parts of Missouri and Illinois.
A few storms today could become strong to severe with large hail and damaging winds the primary concerns.
Hot and humid conditions build Sunday ahead of a more active severe weather pattern Monday and Tuesday.
A more widespread severe weather threat is possible Monday night into Tuesday along a slow-moving cold front.
All severe weather hazards may eventually become possible next week, including damaging winds, large hail, tornadoes, and flash flooding.

An active stretch of late spring weather continues across the Mid-Mississippi Valley today as several rounds of showers and thunderstorms move through the region. Early this morning, thunderstorms developing along a strengthening low-level jet pushed southeastward out of Iowa into portions of northern Missouri and western Illinois. While these storms have gradually weakened, scattered showers and thunderstorms will remain possible through much of the morning hours.

The atmosphere remains quite unstable today with elevated instability values ranging from 1,000 to over 2,000 J/kg of CAPE across portions of Missouri and Illinois. Even though widespread organized severe weather is not expected locally today, any storm capable of intensifying could produce hail up to around one inch in diameter along with isolated damaging wind gusts. The overall tornado threat today remains very low locally, but a collapsing thunderstorm could briefly produce localized microburst winds this afternoon or evening.

The greater organized severe weather risk today will remain farther west across Kansas and portions of the central Plains where a much stronger severe weather setup is unfolding. Supercells capable of very large hail, damaging winds, and tornadoes are expected to develop there later this afternoon and evening. Some of those storms may eventually organize into clusters and drift eastward tonight toward portions of Missouri.

For the St. Louis metro, central Missouri, southeast Missouri, and southern Illinois, today’s severe threat remains more isolated and conditional, but residents should still remain weather aware through the evening hours.

Sunday will likely be the quietest overall day of the extended forecast, although it will also be the hottest. Strong southerly winds and abundant sunshine will push temperatures well into the upper 80s and lower 90s across much of the region. Humidity levels will also continue climbing, making it feel increasingly summer-like across the area. While most locations should remain dry Sunday, the atmosphere will continue loading with moisture and instability ahead of a much more concerning setup early next week.

Monday is when the pattern begins turning significantly more active across the Plains and Mid-Mississippi Valley. A strengthening upper-level trough moving eastward from the western United States will interact with very warm, humid Gulf air already in place across the region. Thunderstorm chances will increase Monday afternoon and especially Monday night as disturbances eject northeastward ahead of an approaching cold front.

At this point, the highest severe weather threat Monday may initially focus across portions of Kansas, Nebraska, Iowa, and northern Missouri. However, the atmosphere across Missouri and Illinois will become increasingly favorable for severe thunderstorms by Monday evening into overnight hours.

Forecast ingredients Monday include:

Strong instability fueled by temperatures in the 80s and dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s
Increasing wind shear capable of supporting organized thunderstorms and supercells
A strengthening low-level jet transporting rich Gulf moisture northward
An approaching frontal boundary serving as a focus for storm development

Depending on exact storm evolution and frontal timing, Monday evening and overnight could feature severe thunderstorms capable of large hail, damaging winds, and isolated tornadoes across portions of Missouri and Illinois.

Tuesday currently appears to hold the greatest local severe weather potential for eastern Missouri and western Illinois as the cold front slowly pushes southeast through the region. Confidence is increasing that multiple rounds of thunderstorms may develop along and ahead of the boundary Tuesday afternoon and evening.

If the front moves through during peak daytime heating Tuesday afternoon, the severe weather threat could become more significant locally. Forecast soundings continue suggesting ample instability and strong enough wind fields for organized severe storms. All severe weather hazards may be possible Tuesday, including:

Damaging straight-line winds
Large hail
Isolated tornadoes
Torrential rainfall and localized flash flooding

One additional concern next week will be the potential for repeated rounds of thunderstorms over the same locations. With very high moisture content in the atmosphere, storms will be capable of producing heavy rainfall rates. This may eventually lead to localized flooding concerns, especially in urban and low-lying areas.

Behind the cold front, cooler and less humid air is expected to arrive by midweek with temperatures falling back into the 60s and 70s by Wednesday and Thursday.

The bottom line: today features a localized severe weather threat, but the more significant and widespread severe weather potential appears to arrive Monday night into Tuesday. Now is the time to review severe weather safety plans and stay updated with future forecast changes as confidence in the details continues increasing.
